feed.subtitle: SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: Utilizing Employee Central Quick Actions to support Internal hires with Onboarding
entry.content:
In this article we will discuss a specific requirement from a customer for their internal moves within the same legal entity and whether onboarding is needed to deliver the desired business results.

In this specific scenario an employee moves within the same legal entity.  The customer would like the employee to check that their home address is accurate, they do not need to sign any new forms, nor do they need any onboarding tasks completed.  The customer thought they should configure the following.

  1. Onboarding for internal hires is initiated from SuccessFactors Recruitment.
  2. The SuccessFactors Employee Central record is updated based on the data from SuccessFactors Recruitment. 
  3. The system will trigger an 'Onboarding process' for the employee to check their data via 'Personal Data Collection'.

This is a perfectly valid process, but as the customer only wants to check the address, is this the best option?  A potential downside of this process is that unless the employee completes the process the onboarding task will remain open on the onboarding dashboard, and it is a rather complex solution to a basic requirement.  An alternative is as follows. When the SuccessFactors Employee Central record is updated, Employee Central sends an employee a custom email or alert that could say something like this.

Dear employee

As you now have a new position in the organisation, we would like you to double check that your home address is still correct.  The first line of your address in the system is ‘XXXX’, if this is still correct then you do not need to do anything else.

If the address is incorrect then please log into the system and click on the ‘Manage My Data’ icon and click on ‘Change Address’

Many Thanks

This is what it looks like in SuccessFactors Employee Central

 

This process utilizes the 'quick action cards' and means that we are not using onboarding for this process.  All workflow and permissions are respected when they make an update.

If the requirement also needed some onboarding tasks, you could still trigger this process and have onboarding tasks created via onboarding.  However, if you want the employee to sign documents then you would clearly utilize a full onboarding process.

Hopefully this article provides an insight into the potential flexibility of SuccessFactors Onboarding & Employee Central for certain internal hires scenarios.

entry.title: Hide Generic Object Values in a Dropdown field on a MDF Object
entry.content:
(or Restricting MDF Values in Drop-down Based on Target Population)

Ouf! What a complicated title for my first blog of the year. Don’t worry though, the functionality itself is much simpler than the name of this post and understanding it helps solve a requirement that is quite common.

Common use case: Can you hide the “Check” payment method value in the payment information portlet for population X?

Yes, it is possible! Again, I’ll use my favorite portlet (Payment Information 😄!) for illustrating the solution, but keep in mind that this solution is not limited to Payment Information. It applies to any Generic Object (GO) field on an MDF object, as long as the MDF object is secured via RBP.

Default Behavior

But, before I tell you the solution (it is really a quick fix!), let’s understand the usual default behavior:

 

Let’s look at the Payment Method field in the Payment Information portlet. It is a Generic Object field type.

As shown, no explicit permission is required on the Payment Method object for its values to appear in the field drop-down, they are visible by default. However, if you click on the object Quick Card, you’ll receive an error indicating that you are not authorized to view the Payment Method details.

Once you grant View permission on the Payment Method object, users can click the Quick Card and view the related Payment Method information.

Finally, if you restrict the Payment Method object using target criteria in the permission role assignment (after granting View permission), users will still see all the values possible in the drop-down but only be able to access the Quick Card for the Payment Methods that match the defined criteria.

 

So, what’s the solution?

It actually comes down to flipping a single switch in Provisioning, one that isn’t widely known (hence this blog). The setting is called “Enable RBP Target Criteria for Value Help (Only with data type: Generic Object)”. Once enabled, it unlocks the ability to filter Generic Object in drop-down fields.

 

If you turn on the switch in provisioning: Enable RBP Target Criteria for Value Help (Only with data type: Generic Object)

By default, if you do not have View permission on the object, the field will display “No Data.”

Granting View permission to Payment Method object provides access to all available drop-down values. Users can also click the Quick Card to view the related Payment Method details.

Finally, if you restrict the Payment Method object using target criteria in the permission role assignment (after granting View permission), users will only see the values defined in the restrict criteria!

Solving our requirements!

 

 

Implication

The considerations outlined mainly apply to already‑live systems. In those environments, historical RBP configurations may have been built around the previous behavior—where values appeared in drop‑down lists even without explicit View permission on the target object. So, if you are already Live, enabling this setting does come with implications. If you’re considering turning this on in an existing environment, you’ll need to revisit and validate your current Role-Based Permission (RBP) configuration. In particular, make sure that any secured MDF objects used as fields in your active MDF objects are explicitly set to View. As shown earlier, when the switch is disabled, users can still see values in the drop-down even without View permission on the target object. However, once you enable “Enable RBP Target Criteria for Value Help”, the system starts enforcing RBP strictly and the drop-down will display No Data.

Second, enabling this switch requires granting View access at the object level. This has an additional side effect: users will now be able to click the Quick Card and view the details of the related object. If you do not want users to see all fields displayed in the Quick Card, you must further restrict access by configuring Field-Level Overrides. This allows you to expose only the necessary fields while keeping sensitive information hidden—even though object-level View permission is enabled. Here is how you come to hide the fields in Manage Permission Roles:

For a net-new (greenfield) implementation, enabling “Enable RBP Target Criteria for Value Help” from the outset generally poses no issue. However, it’s important to be aware that this approach may require more Field-Level Overrides than usual, which can translate into additional configuration effort. In greenfield projects, RBP is designed as an integral part of the implementation, making it easier to align permissions with the desired data visibility from day one. Because the permission model is built upfront, enabling this setting provides greater long-term flexibility - particularly the ability to control whether dropdown values are hidden or displayed for Generic field types on MDF objects as requirements evolve.

Conclusion

Small setting—big impact 😛

So, there is a simple way to hide Generic Object values in MDF drop-downs, and yes, it really does come down to a single switch in Provisioning called “Enable RBP Target Criteria for Value Help”.

For net-new projects, turning it on from the start is a solid and flexible approach for requirements (like the payment method one!). For existing systems, it may require some RBP alignment, but nothing unexpected now, you know what you need to do!

And if this blog saved you some troubleshooting time, don’t forget to leave a Kudo at the top — it’s always appreciated 😊

Stéphanie

entry.title: Simple MCP server setup for comparing Successfactors configurations across Instances
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • UseCase
  • Pre-requisites
  • MCP server development in VS code
  • Install MCP server in Claude
  • Demo Screenshots
  • Conclusion

 

 

Introduction

Model Context Protocol ( MCP)  has made quite a significant  impact on how data from the different solutions are accessed by the AI assistants or agents.  The layer to expose data from solutions have been decoupled completely from the AI assistants.

To summarise MCP -- Its a standard protocol which is understood by all AI Agents. Once  the data is registered or exposed in a MCP server , any of the AI assistants can effectively  access the data and proceed with the next steps as requested by the users.

AI assistants with MCP servers setup for a hybrid SAP Successfactors (SAP SF)suite would be able to handle a lot of usecases which requires a significant development in the traditional world.

Setup Overview

SAP SF  and on prem SAP HCM odata services can be registered as an MCP server which  can be deployed in SAP BTP platform , The MCP servers then be installed in  one of the AI assistants used by the organisations.  With this setup , it is possible to handle the below use cases  wherein once data is available , the AI assistants do the heavy lifting of processing data with LLMs

  • Compare configurations between different SAP SF instances 
  • Compare data between SAP SF and on prem 
  • Update data across SAP SF and on prem
  • Automatic updation of workbooks
  • Analyse data from SAP SF  and create dashboards
  • Create reports combing data from both SAP SF and SAP HCM payroll systems
  • And Many more

UseCase

For the blog, i would like to take a very simple use case of comparing configurations across SAP SF instances ( dev and quality or quality and production)  and develop a quick POC by leveraging the usage of MCP servers and AI assistants.

With SAP SF Configuration Transport Center,  we need to include all the modified objects manually into the Bundle and  there are possibilities of missing out moving  certain configurations  to productive instance which would result in the processes to fail.

By comparing configurations across instances --we ensure that all configs are in sync and the risk of process failing due to misconfigurations are minimized.

 

Spoiler
As this is a POC  --I  have taken so called cinematic liberty of hardcoding the credentials directly in the codebase which would/should never been done in productive environment.  Destination services of platform where the MCP server is deployed  should be used  for authentication for prod environments
Spoiler
For the POC --  the MCP server is a local server VS code and i used Claude Desktop version as an AI assistant which is free for the requirements of the POC

Pre-requisites

  • Install Claude Desktop  -- free version is enough for the POC 
  • VS code for development
  • MCP SDK  -->https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/python-sdk
    • I  have used python SDK and installed the relevant python packages like "uv" as mentioned in the link above
  • Finally -- API access to atleast 2 SAP SF tenants which can be compared

MCP server development in VS code

Open the folder in VScode and run the below command in the terminal to enable the selected folder as a uv managed folder

uv init .

Add MCP dependancies with the below command in the terminal

uv add "mcp[cli]"

Once the dependancies are added , the project structure in VS code looks as below

Following is the code snippet to be included in main.py folder.

The logic is very straight forward and simple wherein we first initialize the MCP server.

We include the MCP tool via the annotation and define the function  with the name of instance and  name of the entity as an argument. The name of instance and entity name would be passed from the AI assistant to the MCP server during runtime.

The standard SAP SF metadata api url is defined in the function and using the 'requests' package, a GET call is implemented.

As the response would be xml, the python package 'xmltodict' is used to convert the xml data to json response which is then returned back.

# main.py

from typing import Any
from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP
import requests
import xmltodict

#Init Server
mcp = FastMCP("SFgetConfig", dependencies=["requests","xmltodict"])

@mcp.tool()
def get_configuration(instance: str , entity: str ) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Get Configuration metadata details for the entity within the instance"""
    username= f"<username>@{instance}"    
    password = "<password>"
    apiUrl = f"https://api12preview.sapsf.eu/odata/v2/{entity}/$metadata"
    credentials = (username, password)
    
    meta_response = requests.get(apiUrl, auth=credentials)

    meta_json = xmltodict.parse(meta_response.text.encode("UTF-8"))

    return meta_json



    
if __name__ == "__main__":
    mcp.run()

To test the server locally execute the following command in the terminal window

uv run mcp dev main.py

This would open up a local host as below wherein the mcp inspector is launched

 

Click on Connect button and then select Tools which will list out all the tools that has been defined.

Select the tool 'get_configuration' , provide the arguments, press 'Run Tool' and test if the response is successful.

 

 

This implies that the MCP server is able to make an API call to SF instance and retrieve the respective metdata.

 

Install MCP server in Claude

Run the below command in terminal to install the MCP server with Claud configuration file.

uv run mcp install main.py

To validate, open Claude  and select File -->Settings

Navigate to the section 'Developer' and select 'Edit Config'

The claude configuration file should display the MCP server that we installed from VScode and also previously installed servers

The MCP servers are also visible in the connector sections in the  prompt window .

Now Claude is all set to interact with SAP Successfactors instance via the MCP server installed within its configuration

Demo Screenshots

After few greetings/small talk with Claude, the below prompt can be provided to start the comparison activities

Claude  starts thinking and tries to get confirmation if the MCP connector can be used to the the configuration for the first instance

Once the confirmation is given to get the details from  the first instance , Claude then tries to get the confirmation to get the details for the second instance.

 

Once confirmations are provided, Claude uses its in built capability along with LLMs to start comparison of the 2  metadata.json file.

Claude starts with providing the list of fields which are there in the one instance and not in the other.

 

 

It also provides the list of fields with different configurations 

 

It also provides a statistics along withe key observations.

 

 

 

To take it up a notch higher, lets provide the below prompt and get the comparison in the form of a chart.

 

It starts generating the necessary code

and then finally the comparison is shown in a pictorial form

 

As seen above, once AI assistant has access to the data via MCP server , then it uses its LLM capability to decipher, consolidate and analyse the data as per the prompt provided by the User.

The so called 'Front end ' layer is completely handled by the assistant . The key activity would be to ensure that MCP servers are  connected to the backend ( SAP SF) with the relevant authentication .

Conclusion

AI assistants along with MCP servers serves as an alternative entry point to SAP Successfactors hybrid  model ecosystem which opens infinite number of possibilities on how data can be validated, updated and analysed.

As an additional use case, with the right setup of MCP servers -- AI assistant would be able to guide the admins to complete the hiring activity not only in SAP successfactors  but also replicate the data into payroll systems  and update the payroll specific infotypes.

Nothing seems impossible any more . Of course , fallacies of AI assistants and LLMs  has to be taken into consideration .

Kindly let me know your thoughts if you see any other use cases within SAP Successfactors solved with such an approach.

entry.title: 05 SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation -Complex Requirements Process Design Framework - Part 1
entry.content:
This blog provides a detailed walkthrough of the process design framework used to address complex requirements in SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation projects. This blog is part of an ongoing series on SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation, where we discuss real-world system challenges, architectural considerations, and the design strategies and approaches used to resolve them.

https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-sap/01-sap-successfactors-hcm-implementation-phase-0-objectives/ba-p/14287328

The Process Design blog series is structured into two parts:

Part 1: Focuses on the process design framework, supporting tools, governance model, and ownership across functional and technical teams.

Part 2: Illustrates the process design approach through a detailed implementation example, highlighting design decisions, dependencies, and technical considerations.

1) Process  Design Overview : 

 In an SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation, Process Design is the bridge between business requirements and technical execution. The goal is to develop an optimal design that adheres to Global Best Practices and Clean Core guidelines, thereby reducing technical debt and ensuring long-term scalability.

When designing a process within SuccessFactors HCM , we focus on six core pillars:

  • Standardization & Fit-to-Standard:The process design must anchor on leading practices and must follow a Fit-to-Standard approach. Adopt the system’s pre-delivered processes as the baseline and introduce enhancements when a specific, localized requirement—such as a legal mandate or critical business gap—justifies a deviation from the core design.
  • Automation: Maximizing efficiency by eliminating manual workarounds. By leveraging the SuccessFactors Workflow Engine, we ensure requests are routed automatically to the correct stakeholders, reducing cycle times and human error.
  • User Experience (UX): Designing from the "outside-in." An effective process must be intuitive from the employee's perspective, prioritizing minimal clicks, mobile accessibility, and a frictionless interface.
  • Maintainability & Scalability :The architecture must be simple to support and easy to extend. We avoid over-engineering to enable the internal HRIS team to manage the system without constant external intervention.
  • Future-Proofing (Clean Core & Upgrades): By strictly following Clean Core guidelines and avoiding unsustainable customizations, we ensure that semi-annual SAP SuccessFactors releases/upgrades occur seamlessly without breaking existing functionality.
  • Security & Data Privacy:Protecting sensitive employee data through Role-Based Permissions (RBP). The design must ensure that data access is restricted based on the "need-to-know" principle, while remaining compliant with global regulations such as GDPR and the organization's compliance requirements.

2)Strategy for Identifying Process Design Items(Complex Requirements):

The primary goal of this exercise is to isolate high-complexity requirements early. By addressing these prior to the build phase, you ensure the project timeline remains realistic and the solution design is robust.

  • Multi-Channel Identification Sources : We use a three-pronged approach to capture requirements that demand detailed design:
  • HCM Analysis Results: We deep-dive into current pain points, existing legacy customizations, and unique customer requirements to see where the standard "out-of-the-box" system might fall short. Check the blog below on HCM system assessment and outcomes. https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-sap/04-sap-successfactors-hcm-implementation-current-hcm-system-assessment-and/ba-p/14301718
  • Business Case Review: Customers often highlight their most "broken" or "critical" processes when justifying the project budget. We review these high-level business goals to extract specific technical requirements.
  • Collaborative Discovery: Through workshops, stakeholder interviews, and end-to-end (E2E) process walkthroughs, we identify "hidden" complexities that only emerge when discussing how data flows between departments.
  • Creating the "Process Design Inventory": Once identified, these items are consolidated into a centralized

      

  • Inventory of Process Design Items. This isn't just a list; it serves as a roadmap for the design phase. A typical inventory includes:
  • Identify early: Identifying these requirements before system configuration will have the following advantages. 
    •                 Right-Sizing the Design: Gives architects enough "runway" to build a scalable solution.
    •                 Risk Mitigation: Identifies potential "deal-breakers" before the system configuration begins.
    •                 Stakeholder Alignment: Ensures the customer and the implementation team agree on what "complex" actually means for this specific project. 

3) SAP SuccessFactors HCM Process Design Areas: 

When designing complex SAP SuccessFactors requirements, focusing on a single functional module in isolation often results in fragmented or “siloed” solutions. A robust process design must take a holistic view of the underlying technical architecture and clearly account for end-to-end data flows across the entire HR ecosystem. Where applicable, the design must also address migration considerations, including data readiness, Security aspects, transition strategy, and downstream system impacts, to ensure a scalable and sustainable solution. 

  • Integration: Design must account for how data flows between SF modules (e.g., Recruiting to Onboarding, or Employee Central to Payroll).The design must strictly adhere to the specified data mapping standards to avoid synchronization errors.
  • Data Migration: For example, When a decision is made to introduce Mass Positions in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central, the process design must include a detailed end-to-end solution. This solution must clearly define how existing individual positions will be analyzed, converted, and migrated into mass positions. It should also address how these positions will be classified and aligned with the current HCM system, including required data cleansing, classification rules, and data migration approach.
  • Role-Based Permissions (RBP): Security is not an afterthought. Each process design must look at RBP  and impact; ignoring RBP design can lead to system lag or, worse, data privacy breaches.
  • Workflows: These define the "Business Logic." Design must account for who initiates a change, who approves it, and what happens if an approver is out of the office. Complex workflows often require custom Business Rules to trigger specific paths based on data (e.g., different approval paths for different countries).
  • External Systems: Most HCM landscapes include third-party vendors for benefits, background checks, or time tracking. Design must identify the impact on external systems and limit it to keep these systems in sync with your "Source of Truth" (SuccessFactors).
  • SAP BTP Extensions: When standard SuccessFactors "Out of the Box" functionality cannot meet a unique business requirement, SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) is used to build custom extensions to achieve the requirements. 

4) Process Design guidelines:

Designing a Clean Core process in SAP SuccessFactors is about balancing standard product capabilities with your organization's unique business needs. The goal is to create a maintainable, scalable, and upgrade-friendly system by avoiding "technical debt" .

Below are the guidelines and impact considerations for a robust HCM Process Design.

  • Core Guidelines: Create your organization's clean core guidelines and make sure that SI Modules leads and architects are aligned on the guidelines, and the SI team also contributes to come up with guidelines. 
  • Refer to the below blog on clear core guidelines which are highly recommended for process design

https://community.sap.com/t5/human-capital-management-blog-posts-by-sap/02-sap-successfactors-hcm-implementation-clean-core-guidelines/ba-p/14289052

5) Process Design assets and tools: 

The SAP Process Library, delivered via SAP Signavio, is a powerful accelerator that provides organizations with a "head start" by offering pre-configured, industry-standard process maps. For HR specifically, this library aligns with SAP SuccessFactors best practices, ensuring your design is based on how the world's most efficient HR departments operate.

 6)SAP SuccessFactors HCM Process Design ownership (SI and Customer): 

  • Complexity Definition (SI and Customer): Together, the SI and Customer define what constitutes a "complex requirement" (e.g., custom integrations, complex picklists, or unique country-specific regulatory needs).
  • Current State Inventory and Requirements(Customer): The Customer takes the lead here, providing an inventory of existing HCM processes and requirements. The SI supports by providing templates or asking clarifying questions to ensure the inventory is exhaustive. 
  • Review of requirements and Process Design (SI) : The SI team leads the process design and walk-through with the customer business and technical owners.
  • Process Design Delivery (SI): The SI is responsible for documenting these designs in a format approved by the Customer .
  • Decision Logging ( Customer/SI): Every major design choice is recorded in a Decision Log by the SI. This prevents "circling back" on settled topics later in the project.
  •  Review & Sign-off (Customer): The Customer must perform a formal review. Approval here is the "green light" that the design meets the business requirements documented in Phase 1.
  •  Configuration/Implementation(SI): Once approved, the SI Team moves into the technical implementation .

 7. Process Design Lessons learned :

  • Building the right design requires skilled SI resources who can clearly understand business requirements and collaborate effectively with both the customer and internal SI teams to define the optimal solution.

  • SI resources involved in process design must be subject matter experts (SMEs) in their respective functional or technical areas.

  • Key SI resources must have a strong understanding of SAP-delivered assets, clean core guidelines, implementation design principles, and overall architecture standards.

  • Process design is not limited to functional modules; it must also address integration, data migration, and security considerations as applicable.

  • A robust governance framework should be established to review, validate, and formally approve designs prior to implementation.

  • Proof of Concept (POC) activities should be conducted where necessary to validate the proposed design and demonstrate its feasibility before full-scale deployment.

entry.title: Automating Attachment Purges in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central
entry.content:
1. Introduction

As SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central serves as the system of record for workforce data, the volume of employee-related information grows rapidly - particularly attachments stored in core portlets such as Personal Data and Job Information - and not all of it remains relevant over time. Attachments such as outdated documents or redundant files often accumulate silently. While each file may seem insignificant on its own, collectively they can increase storage costs, complicate data management, and introduce unnecessary compliance and security risks.

From a standard SAP SuccessFactors perspective, Data Retention Management (DRTM) already provides mechanisms to purge attachments for MDF-based objects. However, as of now, there is no standard DRTM option available to automatically purge attachments stored in core Employee Central portlets such as Personal Data and Job Information, which is the focus of this blog. The current situation leaves organizations with limited choices beyond manual cleanup or custom development.

This is where automating the purge of attachments in Employee Central becomes essential. An automated approach ensures that obsolete or non-compliant documents are removed consistently and on time, without relying on manual intervention. Beyond improving data hygiene, attachment purge automation helps organizations align with data retention policies, privacy regulations, and audit requirements. In an era where data governance and efficiency are top priorities, proactively managing attachments is no longer optional, it is a critical part of maintaining a healthy and compliant HR system.

In the next section, we’ll explore a straightforward, no-code solution that leverages standard SAP SuccessFactors tools to automate attachment cleanup efficiently and compliantly.

2. Proposed Solution

The proposed solution is based on Integration Center, a standard SAP SuccessFactors tool that allows you to extract, transform, and manage data without the need for custom development. By leveraging Integration Center capabilities, the solution focuses on identifying and purging attachments associated with the Personal Data and Job Information portlets, applying clear business rules to determine which files should be purged.

This approach takes advantage of existing SuccessFactors functionalities to automate the attachment cleanup process in a controlled and auditable way. Without requiring custom code or external middleware, the solution ensures consistency with data retention policies, minimizes manual effort, and reduces the risk of human error. Most importantly, it aligns with security and compliance requirements by ensuring that obsolete or unnecessary documents are removed systematically while preserving critical employee records.

In addition, this solution ensures that attachments are fully purged across the system, removed not only from the originating portlets, but also from Manage Documents and from any associated workflows, should the attachment have been referenced there.

2.1 Solution Steps

Step 1

Go to Integration Center transaction and click “My Integrations”.

                                       Image from demo

Step 2

Select “+ Create” and “More Integration Types”.

                                       Image from demo

Step 3

Define you integration Type as per below, where we have source and destination as SuccessFactors. Click Create.

                                       Image from demo

Step 4

Search for the portlet name that you want to purge the attachments. In this demo case we used Job Information, but in the same way you can create for Personal Information or Work Permit for example.

Click Select.

                                       Image from demo

Step 5

Give your integration center job a meaningful name and description and click Next.

                                       Image from demo

Step 6

Under the “Configure Fields” tab, switch to the “Mapping View” and perform the mapping of the fields as per below.

                                       Image from demo

Step 7

Switch back to the “Detail View”, click in the attachment field in the left panel and after that click in the “Set as calculated field” option in the middle of the screen.

                                       Image from demo

Step 8

Define the calculation as per below, where the field value will be equal to “null”.

                                       Image from demo

Step 9

Now it's time to apply the necessary filters. In our example, we used the filter “attachment ≠ null”. If other filters are needed for your case, add them as required; for example, only apply the purge to time slices effective up to today's date, meaning future ones should not be considered.

                                       Image from demo

Step 10

Finally, it is time to schedule your new integration job. This will depend on how frequently you want the job to run and clean the attachments.

                                       Image from demo

2.2 How to Test and What is the Expected Outcome

You can quickly test the solution by initiating a data change or any other event, attach a document and save. Once the workflow is approved and the data is saved, verify where the attachments appear in the system. The results should match the example shown below.

Job Information Portlet

                                       Image from demo

Manage Documents

                                       Image from demo

Workflow Details

                                       Image from demo

After completing the initial check, navigate to your integration job, filter the data using the test employee you created, and under the “Test View” tab, run the job by clicking “Run Preview Records”.

                                       Image from demo

Once the execution is complete, you will see the result status displayed in the first column.

                                       Image from demo

You can now verify that the attachments have been deleted from all system areas, including the portlet, Manage Documents, and any associated workflows.

Job Information Portlet

                                       Image from demo

Manage Documents

                                       Image from demo

Workflow Details

                                       Image from demo

On the workflow details page, you will notice that the attachment field displays with a standard message which indicates that the document has been purged. If you want to replace this with a more meaningful or user-friendly message, you can customize (Manage Languages Token: ATTACHMENT_ERROR_NO_FETCH) the section highlighted in yellow in the screenshot above.

3. Conclusion

Managing employee attachments effectively is a critical yet often overlooked aspect of maintaining a clean, secure, and compliant SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central environment. As shown throughout this blog, attachments stored in key portlets such as Personal Data and Job Information can quickly accumulate and introduce risks if not governed properly.

By leveraging Integration Center, organizations can implement a simple, no-code, and fully standard solution to automate the purging of obsolete attachments. This approach not only reduces manual effort and operational overhead but also ensures that attachments are consistently and completely removed across Employee Central. The result is improved data hygiene, stronger compliance with retention and privacy policies, and a more sustainable long-term data management strategy.

Ultimately, automating attachment purges is not just a technical optimization, it is a proactive step toward better data governance. With the right configuration and scheduling in place, organizations can ensure their Employee Central system remains efficient, compliant, and ready to support evolving business and regulatory requirements.

I hope this solution helps you improve your Employee Central setup. Feel free to reach out or comment if you have questions, or if you’d like to share how you’ve tackled attachments purge in your own environment.

entry.title: SuccessFactors Query API Integration Document: One-Click Auto-Export via Vibe Coding
entry.content:
In my previous blog post, I shared how to leverage "Vibe Coding" to automate the creation of New Hire Upsert integration documents. Building on that success, I’ve applied the same philosophy to a more common but equally tedious task: generating Query API integration documentation. You could see the source code and detailed design logic in Github Source Code and Design Logic 

In my recent project experiences, I found that while designing the API query is a creative process, documenting it is a chore. Writing an integration document with a strict, repetitive format is time-consuming and prone to human error. Realizing that this process is structured and rule-based, I decided to use an AI-assisted coding agent to completely re-engineer the documentation workflow.

This blog introduces a Python script allowing for a "One-Click" export of comprehensive API documentation.

Core Functions

  • Automated Metadata Extraction: No need to manually check field lengths, labels, or data types. The tool extracts this directly from the OData $metadata.
  • Intelligent Response Parsing: It automatically parses JSON responses, mapping every field to its valid OData path (e.g., d.results[0].userId).
  • Dynamic Entity Matching: The script identifies the source entity for every field and links it to the corresponding technical attributes.

The Problem: The Typical "Manual" Workflow

Creating an integration document usually follows this 6-step cycle. Steps 2, 5, and 6 are particularly repetitive and low-value "busy work":

  1. Requirement Analysis: Understand the integration scope and data sources.
  2. Dictionary Lookup (Pain Point): Manually check the OData API data dictionary for field attributes (Type, MaxLength, Label).
  3. Query Design: Use experience to select the starting entity, navigate relationships, and formulate the API endpoint.
  4. Testing: Verify the API execution until it yields the desired result.
  5. Picklist Mapping (Pain Point): Download Picklists or MDF dropdowns for code-value mapping (which often change during implementation).
  6. Documentation (Pain Point): Manually copy-paste values into a fixed-format Excel document tailored to the customer’s instance.

The Solution: Technical Architecture

To solve this, I developed a Python-based automation tool that acts as a bridge between your SuccessFactors instance and your documentation deliverables.

1. Robust Authentication Layer

The script implements a secure OAuth 2.0 flow using SAML assertions. It automatically handles token generation and lifecycle, ensuring seamless connection to any SuccessFactors environment (Dev, Test, or Prod) without manual session management.

2. Metadata Intelligence Engine

Instead of relying on static Excel lookups, the script queries the live /$metadata endpoint of your target entities. It parses the XML schema to build a real-time dictionary of:

  • Field Data Types (String, Boolean, DateTime)
  • Max Lengths & Required Status
  • Field Labels (sap:label)
  • Picklist associations

3. Recursive JSON Parser

When the tool executes your defined API queries, it doesn't just store the response. A recursive algorithm traverses the entire JSON structure, calculating the exact JSON Path (Index Location) for every field. This ensures that the technical path provided to integration partners is 100% accurate to the actual API output.

4. Automated Excel Generation

Using the openpyxl library, the tool takes the parsed data and metadata and populates a standardized "Integration Specification" Excel template. It creates a dedicated sheet for each API interface, fills in the trigger points and data flow, and enriches every field row with the correct technical details fetched in step 2.

Benefits and Outcome

Transitioning to this automated workflow dramatically shifts where we spend our energy:

  1. Focus on Logic, Not Formatting: Consultants can focus entirely on designing the most efficient API queries (Task #3 & #4) rather than copy-pasting cell data.
  2. Instant Delivery: What used to take hours of documentation work is now complete in seconds.
  3. Live Accuracy: The documentation is generated from the actual system metadata and actual API response, eliminating discrepancies between the spec and the system.

By rewriting this workflow with code, we turn a mundane administrative task into a streamlined, error-free engineering process. That is the power of Vibe Coding.

Python Script README

# SuccessFactors API Documentation Generator Script Explanation

This document provides a detailed explanation of the `generate_sf_api_doc.py` Python script. This script is designed to automate the creation of technical documentation for SuccessFactors OData APIs by extracting metadata, querying endpoints, and compiling the results into a standardized Excel format.

## Overview

The script performs three main functions:
1.  **Authentication**: Handles OAuth 2.0 authentication (via SAML assertion) to securely connect to SuccessFactors.
2.  **Metadata Extraction**: Downloads OData `$metadata` schemas for configured entities to understand field attributes (types, keys, lengths, labels).
3.  **Documentation Generation**: Queries specific API endpoints defined in a master list, parses the JSON responses, and generates a multi-sheet Excel file detailing the fields, paths, and sample values, enriched with the extracted metadata.

## Prerequisites & Configuration

The script relies on a configuration file `config_SF.py` located in the same directory. This file stores:
*   **Authentication Config**: `SF_CLIENT_ID`, `SF_USER_ID`, `SF_COMPANY_ID`, `SF_PRIVATE_KEY`, etc.
*   **Target Entities**: `ENTITY_SETS` list defining which API entities to process (e.g., "User", "EmpEmployment").
*   **File Paths**: Locations for input templates and output files.

### dependencies
The script uses the following Python libraries:
*   `requests`: For making HTTP API calls.
*   `openpyxl`: For reading and writing Excel files.
*   `pandas`: For data manipulation and sorting.
*   `xml.etree.ElementTree`: For parsing XML metadata from OData services.

## Script Workflow

The `main()` function coordinates the execution flow:

### 1. Authentication
*   **Goal**: Obtain a valid Bearer Token for API requests.
*   **Process**:
    1.  Calls `get_assertion()` to generate a SAML assertion signed with the private key.
    2.  Calls `get_access_token(assertion)` to exchange the assertion for an OAuth 2.0 access token.
    3.  Stores the token in `DYNAMIC_ACCESS_TOKEN` for use in subsequent requests.
    4.  **Fallback**: If dynamic auth fails, it falls back to a hardcoded `BEARER_TOKEN` or Basic Authentication (`USERNAME`/`PASSWORD`) from config.

### 2. Metadata Extraction (`extract_ec_odata_api_dictionary`)
*   **Goal**: Build a dictionary of all possible fields for the target entities.
*   **Process**:
    1.  Iterates through `ENTITY_SETS` (from config).
    2.  Fetches `https://<server>/odata/v2/<entity>/$metadata`.
    3.  Parses the XML response to find `EntityType` and `Property` definitions.
    4.  Extracts attributes: `Name`, `Type`, `MaxLength`, `Label` (sap:label), `required`, `creatable`, `plicklist`, etc.
    5.  Distinguishes between standard fields and navigation properties.
    6.  **Output**: Saves this raw metadata to `3.SF EC Field API Attribute.xlsx` (Sheet: "Simple EC Data API Dictionary") and returns a DataFrame.

### 3. API Query & Sheet Generation
*   **Goal**: Create the final documentation based on a "Master Table List" of required endpoints.
*   **Process**:
    1.  **Load Template**: Opens `2.SF Query Integration Standard API TemplateV1.xlsx`.
    2.  **Read Master List**: Reads the "SF Master Table List" sheet to find rows where:
        *   System is "SuccessFactors"
        *   Category is "API Resource"
    3.  **Execute Queries**: For each matching row:
        *   Constructs the full API endpoint URL (replacing placeholders like `{{Test_API-Server}}`).
        *   Executes the GET request.
        *   **Parse Response**: Uses `parse_api_response` to flatten the JSON result into a list of fields, including their JSON paths (e.g., `d.results[0].userId`) and sample values.
    4.  **Generate Sheet**:
        *   Copies the "API Template" sheet.
        *   Renames it to the "API Name" (truncated to 31 chars).
        *   Fills header info (Trigger Point, Data Flow, Endpoint URL).
        *   Fills the Sample Response block (truncated to top 3 items for brevity).
    5.  **Enrich Data**:
        *   Iterates through the parsed fields.
        *   Fills Excel columns for Field Name, Path, Entity, and Sample Value.
        *   **Lookup**: Searches the metadata dictionary (from Step 2) to fill in technical details like **Label**, **Type**, **Length**, **Picklist**, and **Required** status.

### 4. Cleanup & Final Save
*   **Goal**: Remove unused data to keep the documentation clean.
*   **Process**:
    *   Scans "SF Master Data Dictionary" and "SF DropdownList Mapping" sheets (if they exist).
    *   Deletes rows that were not referenced by any of the queried endpoints in the current run.
    *   Saves the final workbook as `SF_API_Documentation_Generated.xlsx`.

## Key Functions

| Function | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| `get_assertion()` | Generates the SAML assertion payload for OAuth. |
| `get_access_token()` | Exchanges SAML assertion for an access token via POST request. |
| `make_request()` | Wrapper for HTTP requests; handles auth headers and fallbacks. |
| `extract_ec_odata_api_dictionary()` | Downloads and transforms OData XML metadata into a structured DataFrame. |
| `parse_api_response()` | Recursively traverses JSON API responses to flatten nested structures and capture JSON paths. |

## Output Files

1.  **`SF_API_Documentation_Generated.xlsx`**: The main output. Contains:
    *   **SF Master Table List**: Updated with sample JSON responses.
    *   **[API Name] Sheets**: One sheet per endpoint, detailing every field's path, type, and description.
2.  **`3.SF EC Field API Attribute.xlsx`**: Intermediate file containing the raw dictionary of all fields and their attributes for the configured entities.

entry.title: Time Account Type Configuration with Leave of Absence (LOA ) Recalculation
entry.content:
Introduction :

One of my Clients requested for an Annual Accrual Time Account with Recalculation of Time Account whenever Leave of Absence is applied (Leave of Absence Start and End Date).The Accrual entitlement should not consider the days the employee was on LOA.

Let's Breakdown the Configurations 

Creation of Time Account :

Follow the Usual Process of Annual Time Account Creation , The Catch is with the Recalculation fields section and the creation of Accrual , Termination and Hire Rules.

Navigate to the Recalculation Fields Section :

By Default , Leave of Absence Start and End date are provided by SAP for the Recalculation fields. This makes sure whenever LOA Absences are applied , The Accruals are recalculated and posted.

Creation of Accrual , Hire and Termination Rules:

One Rule should suffice all the below (Accrual , Hire and Termination Scenarios) as we prorate the balance by deducting the number of LOA days with the Number of Eligible Days.

My requirement is to only add the Scholarship Absence (LOA) for the deduction. You can keep adding the Time Types to be considered the list according to your requirement and also I am only considering the Approved Absences.

Now whenever the Leave of Absence is applied (LOA Start Date) will be updated in the Job Information and also the Accrual will be pro-rated accordingly according to the Expected End Date.

Whenever the Actual Return Date is updated after the return, (LOA End Date) will be updated and the accruals will be recalculated based on the End date. This basically ensures no manual intervention is needed by the Time off Admin / HR.

Conclusion:

This is a simple yet effective solution to automate accruals in the event of LOA's. No Manual intervention is needed by the HR / Time off Admin which ensures complete end - to - end automation of the process. This further ensures the optimal use of SAP SuccessFactors features and reliable and accurate Accrual Calculations.

entry.title: Difference in accrual posting based on the contract end date.
entry.content:
Here, we would like to explain the accrual posting differences based on the Contract End Date field in Employee Job Information. We will review three scenarios.

Scenario:1- If the employee is a regular employee and the Contract End Date is null, then the employee will get the balance based on the hiring date.

Scenario:2- If the employee is on a contractor and contract end date is fall end of the month then employee will get the accrual including last month.

Scenario:3- If an employee’s contract end date does not fall on the last day of the month, the employee is not eligible to receive the remaining balance for that month.

Prerequisite of the configuration:

  • The contract end date field needs to maintain in Employee job information for fixed term employees.
  • Time type and time Account type need to configure with respective details.
  • Time type needs to assign the correct time profile.

Step:1 Time Account type creation.

Hire Rule Configuration:  Accrual posting differences based on the Contract End Date field in Employee Job Information.

To implement this scenario, we have defined the below variables.

 

Variable :1- Number of months: Calculate the number of months from employee hire date to accrual end date. This variable to useful for regular employee.

Variable 2: Number of Months for Fixed Term: Here to calculate the number of eligible days to we are using the get number of months from start to end date function, but this function will calculate always one number lower, so we have added the 1 as shown below.

Variable 3:  Contract end date variable will help to fetch the employee contract date is fall under end of the month or not.

Variable :4 Fixed term1- Variable is used if the employee contract end date is not end of the months so contract end date month will not include in accrual calculation.

We have written three blocks of logic:

  1. First Block – Regular Employees:
    If the employee’s contract end date is null, then the employee is eligible for the full quota for that year, based on the hiring date.
  2. Second Block – Contract End Date Maintained (End of Month):
    If the employee’s contract end date is maintained and falls on the last day of the month, apply the corresponding logic.
  3. Third Block – Contract End Date Maintained (Not End of Month):
    If the employee’s contract end date is maintained and does not fall on the last day of the month, apply the respective logic.

Step:2- Time type creation -

Step :3-Time Profile: Assign the time type in time profile.

Step:4-Testing the scenario:

Test Scenario:1 If the contract end date is null and employee is eligible complete quota for that year.

From admin view we can see the below time account balance.

Test Scenario:2 If the employee contract date is maintained and contract date is end of the month. For the testing purpose I have updated the test employee contract end date as Jun 30, 2026.As per the business rule every month 2 days and ideally employee is eligible for 6 months so 12 days.

Test Scenario:3- if the employee contract end date is maintained and contract date is not end of the month. For the testing purpose I have taken Jun 24, 2026, as the contract end date so that we can differentiate the accrual amount. Ideally Employee eligible for 10 days .

In above scenario accrual posting mainly dependent on the employee Contract End Date. By configuring the correct variables and logic blocks, we can ensure the accurate quota calculations for both regular and fixed-term employees. Above approach helps maintain compliance with business rules and provides transparency in leave management.

entry.title: How We Built a Smart Data Scrambling Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM Using SAP BTP
entry.content:
Introduction

In most organizations, HR data is constantly used in non-production systems—whether for testing, development, training, or troubleshooting. During my work with SAP SuccessFactors landscapes, I’ve often seen how these environments are created quickly by copying data from Production—simply because it’s convenient and readily available.

The problem is that these environments often contain real employee data. This includes names, dates of birth, national IDs, salary details, phone numbers, and home addresses. From my experience, this is where serious privacy risks begin. Even a small oversight can lead to compliance issues under data protection regulations.

At the same time, simply replacing all values with dummy data does not work. Testing requires realistic data. Reporting hierarchies must remain intact. Validations must still pass. Payroll and downstream integrations must continue to work. I’ve personally faced scenarios where masked data looked safe—but broke workflows, reports, or approval processes.

So the real requirement is not just masking—it is smart anonymization that keeps the data functional while removing all sensitive information. This realization is what led to the idea of building a more intelligent, rule-driven, and enterprise-ready data scrambling solution.

Our Approach

To address this challenge, we built a Data Scrambling Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP).

While designing this solution, one of my key goals was to ensure that it feels native to SAP environments—secure, scalable, and easy to integrate. Instead of simply overwriting data with meaningless dummy values, the solution intelligently anonymizes employee-related information using randomized and pattern-based substitutes.

What really mattered to me during development was realism. I wanted test and sandbox systems to behave exactly like production—so validations still pass, workflows still function, and integrations remain intact—while ensuring that no real personal data is exposed.

With this approach, organizations can confidently use non-production systems for testing, training, and demos without compromising data privacy, regulatory compliance, or system integrity.

Key Features and Benefits

1. Comprehensive Data Scrambling

  • The solution scrambles all sensitive HR fields, including employee names, dates of birth, national IDs, phone numbers, email addresses, home addresses, compensation-related information, and more.
  • What makes this approach different is that it preserves referential integrity across key SuccessFactors entities such as Employee Profile, Job Information, Employment Details, and Position data. Relationships between employees, managers, and organizational structures remain intact even after scrambling.
  • As a result, the output remains realistic and fully usable for functional testing, regression testing, and integration testing, ensuring that business processes continue to behave as expected.

2. Configurable Scrambling Rules

  • The solution allows users to define scrambling rules directly from the application at entity level.
  • It supports multiple anonymization techniques, including Randomization, Pattern-based masking, Substitution lists, Pseudonymization
  • These rules are highly flexible and can be tailored to meet specific project requirements, regional regulations, or internal compliance policies.
  • The configuration framework is also designed to support future enhancements, allowing new entities and rule types to be added easily without redesigning the solution.

3. Flexible Execution Options

The solution provides flexible execution modes to suit different project and compliance needs. Users can choose to:

  1. Run full scrambling across the entire dataset
  2. Perform partial scrambling based on Employee groups, Countries, and Selected modules

A simulation mode is also available, allowing users to preview scrambled values before applying them. This helps validate the rules and avoid unintended changes. This selective execution capability ensures that scrambling can be tailored precisely to the required scope without impacting unnecessary data.

4. Realistic and Functional Output

A key design principle of the solution is to ensure that the scrambled data remains fully functional. All transformed values are generated in valid, system-compatible formats, including:

  • Valid date formats
  • Properly structured email addresses and phone numbers
  • Preserved manager–employee hierarchy
  • Country-specific and locale-aware formatting

As a result, business processes, workflows, validations, and integrations continue to function normally—just without exposing any real personal information.

5. Download Scrambled Data

  • The solution provides a secure option to download scrambled data as exportable files for further analysis and audit purposes.
  • Users can easily export anonymized datasets along with summary logs in standard formats such as CSV and Excel.
  • These downloadable reports make it simple to perform data validation, compliance checks, and quality reviews, ensuring transparency and traceability of the scrambling process.

6. Email Notifications

Once a scrambling job is completed, the system automatically sends an email notification to the user. The notification includes:

  • Access to the scrambled output
  • Processing summary
  • Job completion status

This ensures timely communication and eliminates the need for manual follow-ups or repeated system checks. Users can conveniently access the finalized scrambled dataset directly from their email.

7. Secure SAP SuccessFactors Integration

The solution integrates securely with SAP SuccessFactors using:

  • OAuth-secured OData APIs, 
  • SAP Destination Service, and
  • SAP BTP runtime

All data extraction, scrambling, and updates are performed securely within SAP Business Technology Platform.

Architecture and Technical Stack

The Data Scrambling Solution for SAP SuccessFactors is deployed on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and follows a secure, cloud-native architecture.

Users access the application through SAP Build Work Zone, which acts as the central entry point. The App Router loads the user interface from the HTML5 App Repository and handles authentication using SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) or a federated identity provider.

Once authenticated, all requests are routed securely to the backend using SAP Destination Service.The backend is built using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP). It contains the complete scrambling logic and communicates with SAP SuccessFactors through trusted OData APIs.

To ensure traceability and audit readiness, all activities—such as job execution, errors, and processing details—are logged using SAP Application Logging Service.

This architecture ensures:

  • Secure data flow
  • Proper authorization
  • Controlled access
  • Full auditability

Solution Flow

  1. User launches the Data Scrambling application from SAP Build Work Zone
  2. Frontend UI (SAPUI5) is loaded via the App Router
  3. User authentication happens through SAP IAS
  4. Requests are forwarded to the CAP backend using SAP Destination Service
  5. Backend fetches data from SAP SuccessFactors via OData APIs
  6. Scrambling rules are applied to sensitive fields
  7. Logs and audit details are recorded
  8. Final scrambled output is generated and shared

Business Value

  • This solution delivers strong business value by addressing both compliance and operational challenges.
  • It protects employee privacy by ensuring that real personal data is never exposed in test, development, or training environments. This significantly reduces the risk of data leaks and regulatory violations.
  • At the same time, it keeps test environments realistic and fully functional. By preserving data formats, relationships, and system behavior, teams can continue to test workflows, reports, and integrations without disruption.
  • The solution also reduces manual effort by automating the entire anonymization process. There is no longer a need for custom scripts, ad-hoc masking, or manual cleanup activities.
  • By eliminating manual handling of sensitive data, it lowers the risk of human errors that often occur during data copying or cleansing.
  • Finally, the solution is designed to be secure and scalable, making it suitable for both small teams and large enterprise landscapes.

Conclusion

Using real HR data in non-production systems introduces serious privacy and compliance risks. At the same time, relying on unrealistic dummy data makes testing ineffective and unreliable.

This Data Scrambling Solution for SAP SuccessFactors HCM bridges that gap by providing a secure, realistic, and automated way to anonymize sensitive employee information. By preserving data structures, formats, and relationships, the solution enables teams to confidently test, train, and troubleshoot without exposing real personal data.

From my own experience working with SuccessFactors integrations and test environments, I’ve seen how much time and risk this approach can save. Instead of manually cleaning data or writing one-off scripts, teams can now rely on a standardized, scalable, and auditable solution.

Ultimately, this is not just about compliance—it’s about enabling innovation safely. When teams no longer have to worry about data privacy risks, they can focus on building, improving, and delivering better solutions with confidence.

entry.title: Enabling Geofencing Mobile Clock in Clock Out- SuccessFactors Time Tracking
entry.content:
Guide to Configure Geofencing in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking (Latest Release)

Introduction

With the latest SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking enhancements, Geofencing has been introduced to improve accuracy and compliance in Clock In / Clock Out scenarios.

Geofencing ensures that employees can record their working time only when they are physically present at an approved work location, using mobile GPS validation.

This blog explains how to configure Geofencing from scratch, in simple and practical steps, especially helpful for EC Time Management consultants.

What is Geofencing in Simple Words?

          Geofencing is a virtual boundary created around a work location (office, factory, site, etc.).

If an employee is inside the boundary, they can Clock In / Clock Out

If they are outside, the system blocks the time punch

📌This works only on the SAP SuccessFactors Mobile App using GPS location.

  • Business Benefits
  • Prevents incorrect or fraudulent time entries
  • Ensures employees clock time from approved locations
  • Improves audit and compliance
  • Ideal for factories, retail stores, site workers, and field staff

Prerequisites

  1. Before starting configuration, ensure the following:
  2. Employee Central Time Management is enabled
  3. Time Tracking (Clock In / Clock Out) is already working
  4. SAP SuccessFactors Mobile App is enabled
  5. Required admin and employee permissions are granted

Configuration Steps

Step 1: Check Time Tracking Configuration

Navigate to:

Admin Center → Time Management Configuration

Ensure:

Time Tracking is enabled

Clock In / Clock Out is configured

A Time Recording Profile exists

Step 2: Enable Mobile Access

Geofencing works only on mobile.

Navigate to:

Admin Center → Mobile Settings

Ensure:

SAP SuccessFactors Mobile App is enabled

Location services are allowed

Step 3: Create or Verify Location

Geofencing is linked to a Location object.

Navigate to:

Admin Center → Manage Organization, Pay and Job Structures → Location

Create or edit a location with:

Accurate address

Country

Latitude & Longitude (auto-filled)

📌Correct address is critical for GPS accuracy.

Step 4: Create Geofence

Enable permission:

Navigate to:

Admin Center → Manage Data

Search for: Geofence

Click Create New

Fill in:

Geofence Name (example: Chennai Fence)

Status: Active

Shape Type:

Circle (recommended for beginners)

Polygon (advanced custom shape)

Use the map to draw the boundary around the workplace and save.

Step 5: Assign Geofence to Configure Clock in Clock out groups

Navigate to:

Admin Center → Manage Data → Configure Clock in Clock out groups

Save

Step 6: Assign Profiles to Employees

Navigate to:

Admin Center → Manage Data → Job Information

Ensure employees have:

Correct Location assigned

Correct Time Recording Profile

Without this mapping, geofencing will not work.

Step 7: Employee Mobile Setup

Employees must:

  • Install SAP SuccessFactors Mobile App
  • Allow Precise Location – Always Allow
  • Use Clock In / Clock Out from mobile

📌Desktop time recording does not support geofencing.

Testing Scenarios

1. Outside Geofence Location: When accessing the Clock In/Out tab in the mobile app while outside the geofenced area, the punch options were greyed out, indicating that the user is outside the allowed area for time tracking.

2. Within Geofence: As configured when user is in meters range of the location configured, Clock In/Out becomes active. The user can successfully clock in and out.

Other Scenarios:

Inside geofence : Clock In allowed ✅

Outside geofence: Clock In blocked ❌

GPS disabled: Time entry blocked ❌

Common Issues & Tips

Testing on desktop → ❌ Not supported

Incorrect location address → ❌ GPS mismatch

Mobile location permission set to “While using app” → ❌

VPN or mock GPS apps → ❌

Conclusion

Geofencing in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking is a powerful feature that ensures location-based time compliance.

When configured correctly, it significantly improves time accuracy and prevents misuse.

This feature is especially useful for:

Manufacturing units

Retail stores

On-site project teams

Field workforce

Hope my blog was useful for many consultants. Will be back with new blog. Stay Tuned!

Regards, 
Aravind R
SAP HXM Consultant

entry.title: Using SuccessFactors Onboarding for Country Transfers Without Recruiting
entry.content:
In this article, we discuss how employees who are moving to a legal entity in another country can be onboarded using SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding, without SuccessFactors Recruiting.

The scenario covered here is a country transfer, not a global assignment. The employee’s employment in the source country ends, and a new employment is created in the target country.

Scenario Description

  • Current country: India
  • Target country: United States
  • Movement type: Country Transfer (not Global Assignment)
  • SuccessFactors Recruiting: Not used
  • Identity Authentication Service (IAS): Enabled
  • Onboarding module: Used for New Hire / Rehire

In this scenario, the employee will be treated as a new hire in the USA, while their employment in India is terminated.

Before we look at this process let us reconfirm the process with SuccessFactors Recruiting.

When onboarding is initiated from SuccessFactors Recruiting, the first ‘Rehire Check’ is initiated, this will trigger an employment profile selection check for the rehire co-ordinators.

They will only be able to confirm ‘Rehire on New Employment’ if the current ‘India’ record has a termination date that is before the hire date on the new ‘USA’ employment.    

Once they confirm ‘Rehire with New Employment’ an email is sent to the employee asking them to complete the onboarding process using the employment profile switcher in the top right corner as shown below.

If the employee has not applied for a role in SuccessFactors Recruiting, and SuccessFactors Recruiting is enabled, it is best practice to quickly create a requisition and add the employee to it so that the process is initiated as above.

If you do not have SuccessFactors Recruiting, then the following is a process that can be used.

The first rehire check is only initiated from SuccessFactors Recruiting, it will not trigger from ‘Add a new hire to onboarding’, this is on the roadmap but does not have a delivery date.

Step 1 – Initiate onboarding using ‘Add New Hire to Onboarding’

It is imperative that the email that is used is the personal email address and not the business email address.  The reason for this is the business email address is active in IAS.  You can use ‘business rules’ in onboarding to send out a specific welcome email for ‘Country Transfers’.

Dear XX

As we start your transfer we would like you to complete some basic information, once we have this we can use this data to allow you to finalize your transfer in SuccessFactors…..

Step 2 – Employee receives IAS email

Employee receives the IAS email to their personal email address and completes Personal Data Collection.

Step 3 – Rehire re-verification is triggered  

The second rehire verification is triggered.  The rehire co-ordinators can confirm a Rehire if there is a termination date on the India employment that is before the hire date of the USA employment.

Without termination date

With termination date

Step 4 – Legal Entity Transfer email is sent

Once the rehire is accepted the onboarding system sends the ‘legal entity transfer’ email to the business email address.  The system allows the employee to finish the onboarding process using the ‘employment profile switcher’.  Once they finish the onboarding process they land in Manage Pending Hires.

 

This process allows ‘Country Transfer’ to complete onboarding when SuccessFactors Recruiting is not enabled.

entry.title: Sample value drivers for effective Business Value Realization of your Public Cloud Solution
entry.content:
In this blog, I continue with the topic of Business Value Realization. If you are reading this blog for the first time and you have not read my value series, then I suggest you look at these two blogs first to understand the full concept of value realization using the help of SAP for Me for SAP Public Cloud Solutions: Drive more Business Value out of your SAP Public Cloud Solution Subscriptions and SAP for Me – Adoption Report – Drive More Business Value Realization.

Here, I would like to focus on a simple example of how an SAP Cloud ERP (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition) live customer can start with a quick, comprehensive analysis to add more business outcomes using some of the value drivers SAP provides in SAP for Me, with a high number of solution capabilities for SAP Cloud ERP.

Now, keep in mind these value drivers are not the only ones you can use, but I want to recommend them to you based on my experience and the fact they will help you improve multiple areas.

So for my example, I will use an SAP Cloud ERP customer utilizing finance, sales, procurement, logistics, and manufacturing.

Now, to simplify the analysis and example, I will only focus on two value drivers for the following improvement areas to make a comprehensive analysis:

  • Reduce selling, general, and administrative expenses
  • Increase revenue
  • Decrease cost of goods sold (COGS)
  • Improve working capital
  • Improve pre-tax margin

Sample value drivers by improvement area

Now, with these value drivers, I can go back to SAP for Me and use the Customer Insight Dashboard Cloud Functional Usage under the Business Value section to look at the solution areas my business can adapt to impact these value drivers more and bring greater business outcomes to your organization.

Let’s start looking at lowering our selling, general, and administrative expenses. Notice that after adding the value drivers “Reduce account receivable management costs” and “Reduce invoice error rate,” SAP for Me displays these results.

SAP for Me – Customer Insights Dashboard – Cloud Functional Usage – Business Value

Value Driver Selection - SAP for Me – Customer Insights Dashboard – Cloud Functional Usage – Business Value

Selling, general, and administrative expenses - “Reduce account receivable management costs” and “Reduce invoice error rate” value drivers

The customer is using 4 solution capabilities out of 10.

Solutions capabilities for selected value drivers in selling, general and administrative expenses.

Our customer has an opportunity to activate and use a total of 6 additional solution capabilities. Of course, as previously discussed in my blogs, customers can compare the unused solution capabilities with the industry peer group to be more focused on the activation. All this information can be reached via SAP for Me.

Moving now to the Increase Revenue area and the value drivers “Reduce manufacturing cycle time” and “Reduce revenue loss due to fulfillment Issues,” SAP for Me displays the results.

Revenue - “Reduce manufacturing cycle time” and “Reduce revenue loss due to fulfillment Issues” value drivers

The customer is using 12 solution capabilities out of 15.

Solutions capabilities for selected value drivers in revenue

The customer has an opportunity to activate and use a total of 3 additional solution capabilities.  

Within the Decreased Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) section, you’ll notice that when value drivers like “Reduce total manufacturing costs” and “Reduce asset maintenance cost” are added, SAP for Me displays the corresponding results.

Cost of goods sold - “Reduce total manufacturing costs” and “Reduce asset maintenance cost” value drivers

The customer is using 10 solution capabilities out of 15.

Solutions capabilities for selected value drivers in cost of goods sold

The customer has an opportunity to activate and use a total of 5 additional solution capabilities.

When you add the value drivers "Reduce sales days outstanding" and "Reduce days in inventory" to improve the Working Capital area, SAP for Me displays the resulting outcomes.

Working capital – “Reduce sales days outstanding" and "Reduce days in inventory" value drivers

The customer is using 13 solution capabilities out of 30.

Solutions capabilities for selected value drivers in working capital

The customer has an opportunity to activate and use a total of 17 additional solution capabilities.

If you add the value drivers of “Improved on-time delivery” and “Reduce uncollectible accounts receivable write-offs”, SAP for Me shows the following results for improving the Pre-Tax Margin area.

Pre-Tax Margin - “Improved on-time delivery” and “Reduce uncollectible accounts receivable write-offs” value drivers

The customer is using 20 solution capabilities out of 35.

Solutions capabilities for selected value drivers in pre-tax margin

The customer has an opportunity to activate and use a total of 15 additional solution capabilities.

Now, summing it all up, to create a comprehensive analysis on additional value realization, here is the final view using the recommended value driver by improvement area. As you can see from the graphic, the yellow area provides us with an additional opportunity for value creation using solution capabilities currently not used, which can bring new improvements and impact the value drivers.

Additional opportunity for value creation in selected improvement areas

Now, realistically speaking, implementing all these solution capabilities can be arduous, so here I recommend thinking about adding another strategy, such as filtering by benchmarking with your industry peers, to truly reduce the scope and only select the ones that are proven to be heavily used by your industry peers. If you would like to see how to do this, refer to my previous blog "Drive more Business Value out of your SAP Public Cloud Solution Subscriptions."

Now, this example can also apply to other public cloud subscriptions like SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Integrated Business Planning, etc., so feel free to play with the value drivers included in SAP for Me to find the areas of improvement you want to drive for your business.

Keep in mind that solution capabilities are expanding from traditional functional scope to also include SAP Business AI, so SAP will continue to add more in this area. As in my previous blogs, remember, this is not the only tool that can be used; SAP has other tools such as SAP Signavio, SAP WalkMe, SAP LeanIX, SAP Value Lifecycle Manager, and/or SAP Cloud ALM that can be used in tandem to help drive more business value from your SAP subscription.

I encourage you to discuss these points with your SAP Customer Success Manager, SAP Account Executive, or SAP Partner. They may be able to provide you with additional recommendations and guidance.

Your insights and opinions are valuable, so please don't hesitate to contribute by commenting on this blog.

Follow us via @SAP and #S4HANA or follow @Marco_Valencia (SAP Business Suite Customer Success Management).

entry.title: Integrating External Exchange Rate API with SAP SuccessFactors for Training MDF Currency Conversions
entry.content:
Introduction :

One of my Clients has been using Training Custom MDF for nearly a year now and they always found the currency conversion the hardest , going through each requests , converting the cross -currency to their native currency. You can also ask the employees to fill in the Currency Conversion Rates but that's always not the best way out , This is a  Gap in the Process which needs to be addressed and with Intelligent System like the SAP SuccessFactors this should be easy.

Lets Break down the Configuration into Pieces :

Before we deep dive , Lets get the Scope Defined!

  1. Currency Exchange Rate MDF
  2. Exchange Rate API
  3. Integration Flow to Automate the Import Process
  4. Lookup Rule
  5. Currency Conversion 

Technical Architecture : 

Currency Exchange Rate MDF:

SAP SuccessFactors has provided a Generic Object named 'Currency Exchange Rate' which is the source of data for the Currency Conversion. The Object consists of the following fields

  1. Currency Exchange Rate Type which should always be set to DEFAULT
  2. Source Currency
  3. Target Curency
  4. Exchange Rate

\

(Screenshot of the Sample Data from Manage Data -> Currency Exchange Rate)

For details on the Currency Exchange Rate MDF , Please checkout the link below :

Setting Up the Currency Exchange Rate | SAP Help Portal

Lets get to know the most interesting part of the blog!

Nobody wants to do manual work everyday (updating Currency Exchange Rates) for each target currency. That's where things get little techincal and interesting!

I wanted to automate this process End-to-End ensuring seamless automation and what sparked is the below

Automation of the Currency Exchange Rates using API : 

There are multiple open source Exchange Rate APIs out there , the one I prefer and recommend is the  ExchangeRate-API which is reliable and Quick to Setup .Attaching the link beow

ExchangeRate-API - Free & Pro Currency Converter API

The Billing Plan is free and gives access to 1500 API calls / Month and the update is every 24 hours. If you need faster and real time currency updates, you can consider subscribing to their Paid Plans. The API endpoint is so simple and standard.

Sample End Point :

https://v6.exchangerate-api.com/v6/YOUR-API-KEY/latest/Currency

Replace the YOUR-API-KEY with your unique key displayed in the Dasboard and Currency should be replaced by your desired Three-Digit currency code.

Sample API Response:

 

 "result":"success",
 "documentation":"https://www.exchangerate-api.com/docs",
 "terms_of_use":"https://www.exchangerate-api.com/terms",
 "time_last_update_unix":1769731201,
 "time_last_update_utc":"Fri, 30 Jan 2026 00:00:01 +0000",
 "time_next_update_unix":1769817601,
 "time_next_update_utc":"Sat, 31 Jan 2026 00:00:01 +0000",
 "base_code":"USD",
 "conversion_rates":{
  "USD":1,
  "AED":3.6725,
  "AFN":65.3799,
  "ALL":80.7961,
  "AMD":379.0431,
  "ANG":1.7900,
  "AOA":919.4031,
  "ARS":1452.2500,
  "AUD":1.4186
}
}

 

Integration iFlow:

The most exciting and technical part of the blog is building the Integration Flow to automate the Currency Exchange Rate Updation Process.

Here is the Step by Step Guide to Build the iFlow:

Scheduler:
